title: Optomechanical system with tunable dissipative and dispersive couplings
authors: Quansen Wang, Yuefan Wu, Doudou Wang, Genyuan Xu, Jiawei Liang, Qiang Zhang, Yongmin Li
abstract: We demonstrate an optomechanical system with tunable dissipative and dispersive couplings using a Fabry-Perot cavity and a string mechanical resonator. By varying the diameter and material of the mechanical resonator, and the relative location between the mechanical resonator and the cavity, the relative strengths of dissipative and dispersive coupling could be tuned continuously from dissipation-dominated regime to dispersion-dominated regime. In our experiments, the dissipative-to-dispersive coupling ratios of 1.3 and 0.6 are achieved by using two different mechanical resonators, corresponding to a transition from dissipation-dominated to dispersion-dominated optomechanical system. Theoretically, the coupling ratio could be tuned from 25 to 0.02 by optimizing the mechanical resonator, spanning over three orders of magnitude. These two distinct coupling regimes are achieved with the same experimental platform. The capability to freely adjust the coupling ratio provides a versatile platform for exploring quantum effects of massive mechanical resonators and quantum-limited measurements.
summary: The paper presents an optomechanical system using a Fabry-Perot cavity coupled to a mechanical resonator, demonstrating unprecedented control over the coupling regime. By manipulating the physical parameters of the mechanical element, the authors can continuously tune the ratio between dissipative and dispersive couplings across several orders of magnitude. This capability makes it a powerful tool for investigating quantum phenomena in massive mechanical resonators.
main_problem: The goal is to develop an optomechanical system capable of continuously tuning both dissipative and dispersive couplings to explore quantum effects.
main_result: The authors demonstrated the ability to tune the coupling ratio from dissipation-dominated ($	ext{ratio} > 1$) to dispersion-dominated ($	ext{ratio} < 1$) regimes using a single experimental platform.
method: The study combines theoretical modeling using transfer matrices with experimental measurements on an optomechanical setup involving a Fabry-Perot cavity and a movable mechanical resonator.
model_or_system: The physical system is an optomechanical device comprising a Fabry-Perot cavity coupled to a string mechanical resonator. The coupling strength is controlled by varying the resonator's diameter, material, and position relative to the cavity field.
key_observables: Dissipative coupling constant ($g_\gamma$), dispersive coupling constant ($g_\omega$), and their ratio ($	ext{ratio} = g_\gamma / g_\omega$).
important_parameters: The theoretical tuning range for the coupling ratio spans from 25 down to 0.02, covering over three orders of magnitude.
main_assumptions: In the advanced analysis sections, assumptions include treating the MR interaction as a 'shadow effect' and approximating beam waists at different points along the cavity.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the schematic setup for tunable coupling (Fig. 1), stability improvement using dual-cavity techniques (Fig. 2), measured trends of linewidth and frequency shift vs. position (Fig. 3), and calculated coupling strengths (Fig. 4).
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical platform and theoretical framework (transfer matrices, coupling definitions) to presenting experimental results showing tunable regimes using different resonators, and finally detailing advanced analytical derivations for field propagation through dielectric media.
why_interesting: This work provides a versatile, highly controllable platform for studying open quantum systems dynamics, allowing precise tuning between dissipation-dominated and dispersion-dominated regimes, which is crucial for ground state cooling research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
